gazette: the United States Court of International Trade
title: Regarding the judicial process on the reciprocal tariffs of the USA
bodyHtml:
On May 28, the U.S. Court of International Trade ruled that tariffs imposed by former President Trump under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) are illegal. The court stated that IEEPA does not grant the president unlimited power to impose tariffs, especially not as a response to trade deficits, which are governed by a separate law—the 1974 Trade Act. The court also found that tariffs related to immigration and fentanyl concerns do not meet IEEPA's requirement of addressing a declared national emergency.

Although the court ordered the government to stop collecting these tariffs within ten days, the ruling has been temporarily paused due to an appeal. For now, the tariffs—some of which may increase significantly in July—remain in effect. 

Please click for the CIT's judgement.

Please click for the appeals court's order.
